tt_insn_vec.help: Portion of vector registers that is actually used. Available only for instructions belonging to a SIMD/vector instructions set (like x86 AVX)

tt_Recip_throughput.help: Average number of cycles to retire that instruction if repeated (assuming independent instructions, with no read-after-write dependency)

hint.details:
Calling (and then returning from) a function prevents many compiler optimizations (like vectorization), breaks control flow (which reduces pipeline performance) and executes extra instructions to save/restore the registers used inside it, which is very expensive (dozens of cycles). Consider to inline small functions.
 - unknown: 7 occurrences<<list_path_1_call_1>>
hint.title: CALL instructions

hint.details:
These instructions generate more than one micro-operation and only one of them can be decoded during a cycle and the extra micro-operations increase pressure on execution units.
 - IDIV: 4 occurrences<<list_path_1_complex_1>>
 - JMP: 12 occurrences<<list_path_1_complex_2>>
hint.title: Complex instructions

hint.workaround:
Use vector aligned instructions:
 1) align your arrays on 32 bytes boundaries: replace { void *p = malloc (size); } with { void *p; posix_memalign (&p, 32, size); }.
 2) inform your compiler that your arrays are vector aligned: if array 'foo' is 32 bytes-aligned, define a pointer 'p_foo' as __builtin_assume_aligned (foo, 32) and use it instead of 'foo' in the loop.
<<image_vec_align>>
hint.title: Vector unaligned load/store instructions
hint.txt: Detected 2 suboptimal vector unaligned load/store instructions.

hint.workaround: Avoid mixing data with different types. In particular, check if the type of constants is the same as array elements.
hint.title: Conversion instructions
Type of elements and instruction set
hint.txt:
6 SSE or AVX instructions are processing arithmetic or math operations on double precision FP elements in scalar mode (one at a time).
8 SSE or AVX instructions are processing arithmetic or math operations on double precision FP elements in vector mode (two at a time).

hint.title: Matching between your function (in the source code) and the binary function
hint.txt:
The binary function is composed of 28 FP arithmetical operations:
 - 21: addition or subtraction (6 inside FMA instructions)
 - 6: multiply (all inside FMA instructions)
 - 1: square root
The binary function is loading 496 bytes (62 double precision FP elements).
The binary function is storing 256 bytes (32 double precision FP elements).

hint.title: Arithmetic intensity
hint.txt: Arithmetic intensity is 0.04 FP operations per loaded or stored byte.

expert.help: Back-end corresponds to macro/micro operations execution (includes allocating registers, fetching operands etc.)
expert.title: Back-end
expert.txt:
| ALU0/BRU0 | ALU1  | ALU2  | ALU3/BRU1 | AGU0  | AGU1  | AGU2  | FP0  | FP1  | FP2  | FP3
--------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------
uops   | 59.00     | 59.00 | 59.00 | 59.00     | 41.00 | 41.00 | 32.00 | 5.00 | 5.00 | 8.00 | 8.00
cycles | 59.00     | 59.00 | 59.00 | 59.00     | 41.00 | 41.00 | 32.00 | 5.00 | 5.00 | 8.00 | 8.00

Execution ports to units layout:
 - ALU0/BRU0: ALU, BRU
 - ALU1: ALU
 - ALU2: ALU
 - ALU3/BRU1: ALU, BRU
 - AGU0 (256 bits): store address, load
 - AGU1 (256 bits): store address, load
 - AGU2: store address
 - FP0 (256 bits): VPU
 - FP1 (256 bits): VPU
 - FP2 (256 bits): VPU, FP store data
 - FP3 (256 bits): VPU, DIV/SQRT

Cycles executing div or sqrt instructions: 104.00-108.00
Cycles loading/storing data              : 34.50

expert.help: Compare the CQA-computed load/store/compute metrics with the capacity of the core. For instance if CQA computes 24 bytes in the loop in 2 cycles (that is 12 cycles per cycle) and if the core can process up to 16 bytes per cycle, that metric is 75%.
expert.title: Cycles and memory resources usage
expert.txt:
Assuming all data fit into the L1 cache, each call to the function takes 108.00 cycles. At this rate:
 - 7% of peak load performance is reached (4.59 out of 64.00 bytes loaded per cycle (GB/s @ 1GHz))
 - 7% of peak store performance is reached (2.37 out of 32.00 bytes stored per cycle (GB/s @ 1GHz))

expert.help: List of bottlenecks detected by CQA. A bottleneck is an issue which reduces front-end throughput below the core capacity.
expert.title: Front-end bottlenecks

header:
Warnings:
 - The number of fused uops of the instruction [CLTD] is unknown
 - Detected a function call instruction: ignoring called function instructions.
Rerun with --follow-calls=append to include them to analysis  or with --follow-calls=inline to simulate inlining.
1% of peak computational performance is used (0.26 out of 24.00 FLOP per cycle (GFLOPS @ 1GHz))

gain.workaround:
- Try to reorganize arrays of structures to structures of arrays
 - Consider to permute loops (see vectorization gain report)
gain.title: Code clean check
gain.txt:
Detected a slowdown caused by scalar integer instructions (typically used for address computation).
By removing them, you can lower the cost of an iteration from 108.00 to 17.83 cycles (6.06x speedup).

gain.workaround:
- Try another compiler or update/tune your current one
 - Make array accesses unit-stride:
  * If your function streams arrays of structures (AoS), try to use structures of arrays instead (SoA):
for(i) a[i].x = b[i].x; (slow, non stride 1) => for(i) a.x[i] = b.x[i]; (fast, stride 1)
gain.details:
25% of SSE/AVX instructions are used in vector version (process two or more data elements in vector registers):
 - 15% of SSE/AVX loads are used in vector version.
 - 12% of SSE/AVX stores are used in vector version.
 - 87% of SSE/AVX addition or subtraction instructions are used in vector version.
 - 20% of SSE/AVX fused multiply-add instructions are used in vector version.
 - 0% of SSE/AVX divide and square root instructions are used in vector version.
 - 26% of SSE/AVX instructions that are not load, store, addition, subtraction nor multiply instructions are used in vector version.
Since your execution units are vector units, only a fully vectorized function can use their full power.
gain.title: Vectorization
gain.txt:
Your function is poorly vectorized.
Only 26% of vector register length is used (average across all SSE/AVX instructions).
By fully vectorizing your function, you can lower the cost of an iteration from 108.00 to 14.50 cycles (7.45x speedup).

gain.workaround:
- Reduce the number of division or square root instructions:
  * If denominator is constant over iterations, use reciprocal (replace x/y with x*(1/y)). Check precision impact. This will be done by your compiler with ffast-math or Ofast
 - Check whether you really need double precision. If not, switch to single precision to speedup execution
gain.title: Execution units bottlenecks
gain.txt:
Performance is limited by execution of divide and square root operations (the divide/square root unit is a bottleneck).

By removing all these bottlenecks, you can lower the cost of an iteration from 108.00 to 79.83 cycles (1.35x speedup).

potential.title: Expensive FP math instructions/calls
potential.txt:
Detected performance impact from expensive FP math instructions/calls.
By removing/reexpressing them, you can lower the cost of an iteration from 108.00 to 79.17 cycles (1.36x speedup).
